aliases: 蒜香辣椒意大利面 (Suànxiāng Là Jiāo Yìdàlì Miàn)

chinese: 蒜香辣椒意大利面 (Suànxiāng Là Jiāo Yìdàlì Miàn)

region_of_origin: Italy (Naples / Southern Italy); widely served in Singapore Western cafés
food_subgroup: Italian pasta with olive oil dressing; cooked spaghetti tossed in garlic-infused extra-virgin olive oil with chilli flakes, parmesan, and parsley

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for spaghetti aglio olio as a specific dish. Closest parent is a generic pasta dish concept. URI left null to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta, cooked — FoodEx2 base term for cooked pasta dishes; used as closest defensible anchor for spaghetti aglio olio composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific aglio olio composite code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for spaghetti aglio olio. Left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 250 g cooked spaghetti, 40–56 g extra-virgin olive oil, and 20 g grated parmesan, as typically served in a Singapore Western café.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti Aglio Olio — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite calculation (cooked spaghetti, olive oil, garlic, parmesan, parsley)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by composite calculation from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked enriched spaghetti (FDC ID 20124), olive oil (FDC ID 04053), raw garlic (FDC ID 11215), parmesan cheese (FDC ID 01032), and fresh parsley (FDC ID 11297), weighted to a 300 g serving ratio of approximately 250 g spaghetti + 40 g olive oil + 20 g parmesan + 5 g garlic + 5 g parsley, then expressed per 100 g of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; spaghetti (white, boiled 10–15 min) GI = 46 ± 4; al dente spaghetti GI range 41–61
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 52 is the mid-range estimate for al dente white spaghetti from the University of Sydney GI Database (published range 41–61 across multiple studies). The olive oil in aglio olio slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response, supporting use of the lower-to-mid range value. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (52 × 66 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 34.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~140 mg/100 g; ~420 mg per 300 g serving), predominantly from parmesan cheese; olive oil contributes no sodium
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 300 g serving, spaghetti aglio olio is one of the lower-sodium Western café pasta options and is acceptable within a hypertension-management diet targeting under 2000 mg sodium per day, provided parmesan quantity is not increased.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~34 per 300 g serving) from a substantial pasta carbohydrate portion (66 g carbohydrate per serving), partially mitigated by olive oil slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a relatively low GI (~52 for al dente spaghetti), the 300 g serving delivers approximately 66 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~34, which is significant for blood glucose management; people with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g) or pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat profile from extra-virgin olive oil (~6 g MUFA per 100 g; ~18 g MUFA per 300 g serving); low saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g); oleocanthal anti-inflammatory properties of EVOO
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The extra-virgin olive oil base provides a cardioprotective MUFA-dominant fat profile consistent with Mediterranean dietary patterns, with low saturated fat (4.5 g per serving) and no trans fat, making this dish suitable for cardiovascular disease management when consumed in moderate portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: EVOO oleocanthal anti-inflammatory effect; garlic allicin modest anti-inflammatory properties; very low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The EVOO-dominant fat profile (oleocanthal) and garlic (allicin) provide modest anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, and this dish is suitable as an occasional Mediterranean-style meal for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Aglio Olio (蒜香意面) is one of Italy's simplest and most traditional pasta dishes, originating from Naples and Southern Italy. It consists of al dente spaghetti tossed in hot garlic-infused extra-virgin olive oil (EVOO), dried chilli flakes, grated parmesan cheese, and fresh flat-leaf parsley. The dish is known for its aromatic garlic and subtle heat from the chilli, with the rich, fruity notes of olive oil coating the pasta strands to create a silky, lightly glossy texture. In Singapore Western cafés, a standard serving is approximately 300 g and typically uses around 4 tablespoons (40–56 ml) of olive oil per plate, making it calorie-substantial despite its simple appearance. Per 300 g serving: approximately 549 kcal, 15 g protein, 27 g fat (predominantly MUFA from EVOO), 66 g carbohydrate, and 420 mg sodium. The fat profile is cardioprotective — EVOO contributes approximately 18 g monounsaturated fat per serving with negligible saturated fat. GI is approximately 52 (low-to-medium for al dente spaghetti); the olive oil content slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 300 g serving is approximately 34, which is moderate-to-high and relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium is relatively low compared to cream-based or tomato-sauce pastas. Halal and vegetarian. Suitable for cardiovascular health and rheumatoid arthritis as an occasional Mediterranean-style meal. Not suitable for gluten-free or dairy-free diets.
common_user_queries: garlic olive oil pasta calories Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from a USDA SR Legacy composite calculation across five component ingredients weighted to a standard Singapore café serving ratio. No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for spaghetti aglio olio as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for al dente white spaghetti (41–61). Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact olive oil and parmesan quantities vary by café and no Singapore HPB direct entry exists for this dish.
notes: 1. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of the composite aglio olio dish (accounting for pasta water absorption during boiling and oil coating) is not directly reported in USDA SR Legacy for this specific composite preparation; detailed moisture data for this dish formulation is unavailable in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-13. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — spaghetti, olive oil, garlic, and parsley contain negligible vitamin D; parmesan contributes a trace below detection threshold, confirmed zero for this composite. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — extra-virgin olive oil and pasta contain no industrially produced trans fats per USDA SR Legacy. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (6.0 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.0 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy olive oil fatty acid profile weighted to the composite dish ratio. 5. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for al dente white spaghetti (published range 41–61); the mid-range value is used given the fat-mediated gastric emptying effect of olive oil. 6. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (52 × 66 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 34.3. 7. Two portions are noted in the source record: 300 g (verified standard) and 350 g (GrabFood platform observation); 300 g is used as the primary serving for GL calculation. 8. This dish is halal and vegetarian but not vegan (contains parmesan) and not gluten-free (contains wheat spaghetti).
